input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a 1/10-scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology. Its main hardware layers consist of lower and upper level chassis components and autonomy elements. The operational domain is Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) within Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS), supporting real-time monitoring, computation offloading, and remote management capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three core services: Data Monitoring (real-time visualization of vehicle state data, projected driving paths, detected object tracks, data saving, and report generation), Computation Offloading (offloading ACC system processing—lead vehicle detection, speed calculation based on distance, and actuation commands—to cloud resources), and Remote Management (enabling/disabling ACC, setting desired speed, and triggering emergency braking commands remotely). Target users are operators for system monitoring and management, and developers for troubleshooting using data collection capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation utilizes Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware for communication between components, NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the Software Development Kit (SDK) for hardware acceleration, and a SQL database for storing object tracks and ego vehicle state data.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system enforces a 100 ms clock synchronization accuracy for coordination between devices to support computation offloading in safety-critical vehicular applications, constrained by hardware limitations as per industry standards.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system comprises three hierarchical entities deployed on separate hardware components: Physical Twin Entity (handling resource-specific functionality and automotive tasks like Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking), Digital Twin Entity (managing data processing and control mechanisms), and User Entity (split into 3D Visualization and Control Panel subcomponents). Each entity is implemented as individual ROS2 packages with nodes, establishing a decentralized architecture where functionality ownership is distributed across hardware.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The architecture adheres to ISO 23247 RA, mapping software components to Functional Entities implemented as ROS2 packages. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes, organized into Physical Twin Entity (deviating from ISO 23247 by hosting general automotive functionality), Digital Twin Entity (data management), and User Entity (UI components). This structure enables coordinated execution across distributed hardware.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models include Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking using DBSCAN for raw LiDAR point cloud clustering and Kalman filter for object tracking (behavioral/analytical models), and Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) for lead vehicle detection and speed computation. Data management involves storing object tracks and ego vehicle state in a SQL database.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Physical actuators include a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller driving a Motor and Steering Servo, with the Actuation Functional Entity converting desired speed commands into motor voltage signals.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing infrastructure comprises a Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR for 3D point cloud acquisition and Intel RealSense D435i depth camera for stereo vision, with data inputs visualized in Figure 2.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Raw LiDAR point cloud data, vehicle state parameters, and object tracks are transmitted from physical sensors to the Digital Twin via Data Pre-Processing FE and Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking components. Data Translation serializes data streams, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions. Communication between software components occurs over ROS2, while inter-entity data flow uses Wi-Fi with UDP transport layer. Data is processed in real-time for visualization and monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands for adaptive cruise control (ACC), motor actuation, and steering servo are generated by the Controlling FE and transmitted via ROS2 network to Actuation FE components. Data Translation handles serialization and unit conversion of control messages. Inter-entity communication for command delivery uses Wi-Fi UDP protocol. Control signals are processed by Actuation FE to drive physical actuators.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication between software components uses ROS2, while inter-entity communication occurs over Wi-Fi using UDP transport layer. Network latency is maintained at most 100 ms (R7) and clock synchronization accuracy is 100 ms (R8) to ensure real-time synchronization.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Data is stored in a SQL database on a device external to the vehicle. Entities are deployed on separate hardware components, including NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano, with scalability achieved through distributed processing and external storage for data persistence.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin system supports decision-making through the Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) functionality, which processes sensor data to determine lead vehicle presence and compute a desired speed. The user interface enables real-time monitoring, data saving, and report generation of vehicle state information for operational insights.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system adheres to ISO 23247:2021, a manufacturing-specific framework with four parts: Overview and general principles, Reference architecture, Digital representation of physical manufacturing elements, and Information exchange. The reference architecture (Part 2) employs an entity-based structure where Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs) are physically observable items (e.g., equipment, facilities), Data Collection and Device Control Entities handle data acquisition and control of OMEs, Digital Twin Entities provide digital representations of OMEs, and User Entities offer interfaces for monitoring and control. Functional Entities (FEs) within each entity handle specific tasks such as actuation, data transformation, or user interfacing, with OPC Unified Architecture cited as a potential implementation for FEs.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description is highly specific, accurately identifying the physical twin as a '1/10-scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle' retrofitted with autonomy technology. It correctly details the hardware layers ('lower and upper level chassis') and the operational domain ('Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) within Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)'). This is strongly supported by the source text.

The extraction accurately details the three core services: Data Monitoring (viewing vehicle state, projected path, object tracks), Computation Offloading (offloading ACC logic to DT/cloud), and Remote Management (enabling/disabling ACC, setting speed, emergency braking). It also correctly identifies the target users (operators and developers). The details match the source text precisely.

The extraction correctly identifies 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2)' as the middleware and 'NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0' as the SDK. It also correctly states that a 'SQL database' is used for storing 'object tracks and ego vehicle state data'. The description is specific and directly supported by the text.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

block_2_judge.reasoning: The description accurately reflects the specific clock synchronization requirement and the rationale behind it found in the DTS requirements. It correctly cites the 100 ms accuracy, the need for coordination/computation offloading, and the hardware limitations. Quotes: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms.', 'We require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.', 'To enable computation offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.'

The description accurately captures the system's hierarchical structure, the specific roles of the three entities (Physical, Digital, User), and their deployment on separate hardware components. It correctly identifies the decentralized nature and the implementation using ROS2 packages. Quotes: 'Considering that each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component', 'The Physical Twin Entity... can host general automotive functionality, such as Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking.', 'User-facing components are organized into the User Entity... 3D Visualization and Control Panel components.'

The description correctly summarizes the architectural approach, adherence to ISO 23247, mapping to Functional Entities (FEs), and the specific deviation regarding the Physical Twin Entity. It effectively describes the coordination and component organization. Quotes: 'We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design phase.', 'The existence of this entity marks a departure from the ISO 23247 RA... hosts general automotive functionality', 'SW components that implement data management and control mechanisms are organized into the Digital Twin Entity.'

The document describes the internal architecture and communication of the Digital Twin System (DTS) using ROS2 and Wi-Fi between its own entities (Physical, Digital, User). It does not discuss integration with external enterprise systems, other Digital Twins, or specific interoperability standards for data exchange outside the defined DTS scope. Therefore, stating it is 'Not in Document' is correct based on the provided text.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately reflects the source text, detailing the specific algorithms (DBSCAN, Kalman filter), the ACC logic (lead vehicle determination, speed computation), and the data storage method (SQL database). Quotes: 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds via DBSCAN, then tracks using a Kalman filter', 'ACC Determines lead vehicle and computes following speed', 'Data Storage Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database'.

The description is specific and well-supported by the text, identifying the Vedder ESC, the motor and servo actuators, and the specific function of the Actuation FE. Quotes: 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller', 'Actuation Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands', 'Motor', 'Steering Servo' (from Figure 2 representation).

The extraction correctly identifies the Hokuyo LiDAR and the Intel camera. However, the source text refers to the camera as 'Intel Deepsense D435i' rather than 'RealSense', and describes it as a 'Depth Camera' without explicitly stating 'stereo vision' or '3D point cloud acquisition' (though LiDAR point clouds are mentioned elsewhere). Quotes: 'Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR', 'Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', which is accurate based on the provided text. While the document mentions a general definition of DTs as 'real-time and high-fidelity computational reflections' and specifies network latency requirements (e.g., 'maintain network latency of at most 100 ms'), it does not detail model fidelity requirements, validation methods, uncertainty quantification, or error detection mechanisms as defined in the characteristic description.

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. This extraction is highly accurate and well-supported by the source text. In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.

It mentions using ISO 23247 as a starting point, but lacks the depth required by the characteristic description.

The document mentions the 'safety-critical nature of the system' and references ISO 15622, but it does not describe specific security measures such as cybersecurity protocols, access control, or authentication systems.
